comment_text.snp4.8:1.3: Norman has “good” for subha, Bodhi “excellent”, but the Niddesa shows that the sense is “illuminating, bringing wisdom”. The same phrase occurs at Snp 4.13:16.3. Perhaps these are ascetics who rely on the subhavimutti, the “beautiful release” (of jhānas). But compare the “ultimate splendor” (paramo vaṇṇo) propounded by the wanderer Sakuludāyī, which seems to have just an idea without any real basis (MN 79:9.3).
comment_text.snp4.8:1.4: Here, as at Snp 4.12:15.3, puthu emphasizes how they all have their own distinct view.
comment_text.snp4.8:2.2: Dahanti is to “regard” or “see”, per Niddesa and Commentary; Bodhi’s “accuse” seems hard to justify.
comment_text.snp4.8:10.3: The Sanskrit here has the name Sīhaśūra.
comment_text.snp4.8:11.3: Here, yuga is connected in the Niddesa with yugaggāha (“taking the reins”), which in turn is connected in Vb 17:75.2 with “causing disputes”. It seems to be an idiom for “issuing a challenge”.
comment_text.snp4.8:11.4: Sakkhasi is 2nd future, sampayātave is infinitive. Sampāyati is a fairly common word, “answer a question”, usually in a negative sense, to be “stumped” by a question. Oddly, most translators (Bodhi, Norman, Ñāṇadīpa, Ṭhānissaro) don’t catch this sense.

root_text.snp4.8:7.4: Na hi tena suddhiṁ kusalā vadanti.
root_text.snp4.8:9.3: Te tvaṁ vadassū na hi tedha atthi,

translation_text.snp4.8:1.2: denying that there is purification in other teachings.
translation_text.snp4.8:1.3: Speaking of the beauty <j>in that which they depend on,
translation_text.snp4.8:1.4: each one is dogmatic about <j>their own idiosyncratic interpretation.
translation_text.snp4.8:2.1: Desiring debate, they plunge into an assembly,
translation_text.snp4.8:2.2: where each takes the other as a fool.
translation_text.snp4.8:2.3: Relying on others they state their contention,
translation_text.snp4.8:2.4: desiring praise while claiming to be skilled.
translation_text.snp4.8:3.1: Addicted to debating in the midst of the assembly,
translation_text.snp4.8:3.2: their need for praise makes them nervous.
translation_text.snp4.8:3.3: But when they’re repudiated they get embarrassed;
translation_text.snp4.8:3.4: upset at criticism, they find fault in others.
translation_text.snp4.8:4.1: If their doctrine is said to be weak,
translation_text.snp4.8:5.1: When these disputes come up among ascetics,
translation_text.snp4.8:5.4: for the only purpose is praise and profit.
translation_text.snp4.8:6.1: But if, having declared their doctrine,
translation_text.snp4.8:6.2: they are praised there in the midst of the assembly,
translation_text.snp4.8:6.3: they laugh and gloat because of it,
translation_text.snp4.8:7.2: yet they speak from conceit and arrogance.
translation_text.snp4.8:7.3: Seeing this, one ought not dispute,
translation_text.snp4.8:7.4: for those who are skilled say this is no way to purity.
translation_text.snp4.8:8.1: As a warrior, after feasting on royal food,
translation_text.snp4.8:8.2: goes roaring, looking for someone to fight—
translation_text.snp4.8:8.3: go off and find an opponent, Sūra,
translation_text.snp4.8:8.4: for here, as before, there is no-one to combat.
translation_text.snp4.8:9.1: When someone disputes about a view they’ve adopted,
translation_text.snp4.8:9.3: say to them, “Here you’ll have no adversary
translation_text.snp4.8:9.4: when a dispute has come up.”
translation_text.snp4.8:10.1: There are those who live far from the crowd,
translation_text.snp4.8:10.3: Who is there to argue with you, Pasūra,
translation_text.snp4.8:10.4: among those who grasp nothing here as the highest?
translation_text.snp4.8:11.1: And so you come along speculating,
translation_text.snp4.8:11.2: thinking up theories in your mind.
translation_text.snp4.8:11.3: Now that you’ve challenged <j>someone who is cleansed,
translation_text.snp4.8:11.4: you’ll not be able to respond.

variant_text.snp4.8:6.3: unnamatī ca → unnamaticca (bj); uṇṇamatic (pts-vp-pli1); uṇṇamatī (csp1ed, csp2ed)

reference_text.snp4.8:4.1: mc0.497, ms18Sn_1002, ndp20.397, pts-vp-pli162, vnp827, vns834
